macro_segments.title: Introduction to Matnat Sh'chiv Mei'ra and Diatiki (Written Will)

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and orientation to Mishnah on Daf 135b
Definition and mechanism of Matnat Sh'chiv Mei'ra—oral deathbed bequest
Written will (Diatiki) as distinct form—requirement of meshichah (delivery) for binding effect
Mishnah: will bound to thigh not yet binding; binding upon meshichah to any recipient
Distinction between Diatiki and Shtar Matana (gift document)—effective dates and mechanics

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Definition of Diatiki and Matna, and Healthy-Person Gift Approximating Deathbed Bequest

micro_segments.title: Tanu Rabbanan: Diatiki defined by language 'da tehei lemeikam'—established as last will and testament
Distinction between Matnat Bari (healthy-person gift) and Shtar Matana; mechanics of Kinyanim
Abaye's explanation: Matnat Bari she-hi k'Matnat Sh'chiv Mei'ra—gift by healthy person approximating deathbed gift
Me-hayom u'l'achar Misa structure—today title transfers, Peiros retained until death
Tosafos on Matnat Bari: can donor retract? Rashbam vs. Rabbeinu Tam on retraction rights

macro_segments.title: Rava bar Rav Huna's Teaching on Shichiv Mei'ra Who Orders Writing and Delivery of Document After Death

micro_segments.title: Shichiv Mei'ra says 'kitvu u'tnu'—write and deliver will to recipient; dies before completion
Question: Is verbal gift effective or only if written will delivered? Shema lo gamar le-haknoto ela bi-shtar
Rebbi Eliezer's stringency: Izharu bah—be careful about this Halacha; variant attributions debated
Rav Yochanan's qualification: tybadek—must examine circumstances; Mi-yafes es kocho interpretation
Rav Dimi's teaching: two rules—later will overrides earlier (Deyateki mevateles); context assessment
Roiin circumstances; if mi-yafes kocho—extra strength for verbal gift—can write afterward; if unclear, cannot
Rav Chisda on Kinyan: Ukanina minei mosif amatanata—explicit declaration in Shtar of Kinyan
Ruling: Kotvin u'nosnin—can write after death if structured as additional evidence to verbal gift

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Me-hayom u'l'achar Mita Structure: Father Transferring to Son, Rights and Restrictions

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Ha-kosev nechasav le-banav—must write me-hayom u'l'achar misa (Rebbi Yehuda)
Rashbam context: Bari protecting assets before second marriage—transfer title, retain Peiros
Rebbi Yossi's position: Ain tzarich—me-hayom is not required if le-achar misa stated
Rights and restrictions: Father cannot sell principal (kesuvim le-ben); Son cannot sell (birshus ha'av)
If father sells: transfer of right to Peiros only, effective until death (mechura ad she-yamut)
If son sells: buyer gets nothing until father dies; after father's death, buyer owns full Peiros

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Me-hayom u'l'achar Misa: Parsing and Effectiveness of the Formula

micro_segments.title: V'chi kasav me-hayom u'l'achar misa mai havay?—How does this formula work and take effect?
Comparison to Get: me-hayom u'l'achar mita—in Dinei Mammonos can mean title today, Peiros after death
Rav Yosef: Ain tzarich me-hayom—date in Shtar itself testifies; me-hayom is implicit
Anecdote: Rabba bar Avuha ill; Rav Huna asks Rav Nachman about Halacha ke-Rav Yosef
Ta'ama de-Rav Yosef: Zmano shel Shtar mochi'ach—date proves intent; why date unless effective today?
Tanui nami hachi: Beraita supports Rebbi Yossi; Halacha like Rav Yosef (no need for me-hayom)

macro_segments.title: Debate on Kinyan in Shtar: Whether Double Language of Kinyan Indicates Me-hayom Intent

micro_segments.title: Be-hakna mahu?—Does Kinyan in Shtar negate need for me-hayom declaration?
Amar lei be-hakna ain tzarich—By Kinyan, me-hayom not required; Rav Pappa's nuance
Rav Pappa: Ika Akna'ata de-tzarich v'ika de-lo tzarich—Depends on phrasing order of Kinyan language
Akneiyei u'kanina minei vs. Kanina minei v'akneiyei—Order of Kinyan language affects meaning
Rashbam explanation: Double language indicates extra oomph (miyapes kocho); single statement does not
Rav Chanina's challenge: Do we in Beis Medrash really parse such fine distinctions? Scribes knew the difference
Proof: Scribal expertise consulted—Abaye's and Rava's scribes confirmed the order-based distinction
Rav Huna bar Rav Yehoshua's synthesis: Either order with Kinyan = me-hayom; debate applies to non-Kinyan Shtaros
Beduchran pisgamei: Debate of Rebbi Yehuda and Rebbi Yossi is when Shtar does not say we did Kinyan
Rav Kahana's report of Rav Zevid and Rava's clarification: Beaknoi ain tzarich always means me-hayom

topical_tags.term: Shichiv Mei'ra kitvu u'tnu (deathbed order to write and deliver)
Deyateki mevateles deyateki (later will overrides earlier)
Akneiyei u'kanina minei (order of Kinyan language)
Mechura ad she-yamut (sale of Peiros until death)
